i was important!!! i was so important to you i fucking know i was and i know you really fucking cared about me and you were grateful for me and you missed me and you said you'd never turn your back on me no matter what happened. you said i changed your life for the better you told me we would always be there for each other i need you to come back. PLEASE i need you to come back. it’s so lonely here. i am not so important anymore. i am not important!!! nothing is funny anymore. the world is so dull without you in it.
the dead are changeless. you’re frozen, and you’ll always be exactly where you were, but life keeps propelling me forward and i don’t know what to do. we are already so far apart.

I was sad because I wasn’t better. And I’m lonely because I’m still not.

I don’t care that time is passing—I want it to pass faster. I want to skip this part.
i wrote that two days before you passed.

i miss you. i’m crawling on my hands and knees across time like some idiot, trying to get to you. i look at pictures of us, but it’s not you in the photo. just some last-minute actor with your face, your clothes, your smile—but i can tell it’s not you. your heart was so close to mine, i forgot you even had a face.

if you looked like how you felt, they'd see right through you. because you are willingly transparent, but you are not honest—and you speak in selective disclosures. people mistake the first for the second all the time; we believe anything that looks like pain. visibility plays well, but the truth can be opaque under it.
if i were an animal i'd be a parastratiosphecomyia stratiosphecomyioides
if you aren’t capable of this, then what are you capable of?

dear god,
there are bees in my ears. not metaphorically. this is real. also i’m in a bear suit. i don’t remember how it started. a bit maybe. a party? either way, the zipper is stuck. and it’s hot—the kind of heat you first rationalize, then spiritualize, then quietly accept as punishment.
the suit wasn’t assigned. i put it on. i thought if i looked the part—if i wore something heavy and public and clearly uncomfortable—someone might believe i was sorry. or at least that i knew i should be.
inside the suit it’s just me and the bees. they hum, buzz, and quote scripture. you were warned. obedience would’ve been wiser. total depravity isn’t a metaphor. you are irredeemable. i tell them to shut the fuck up. they don’t know anything about me.
people tiptoe around me like i might maul them. they assume i’m a bear. i keep hoping someone will unzip the back and say oh. it’s just you. you’re not a real bear and therefore you’re not damned. but the bees are too loud.
and fine, i put them there too. i thought buzzing would be easier than doubt. doubt is too quiet. doubt leaves room. the bees don’t. they fill my head with noise and verses and warnings. it’s awful, but on some level there is comfort in certainty.
i thought if i wore the guilt long enough, if i looked like i felt it, if i repented correctly, repeatedly, visibly, someone—maybe even you—might hand me a certificate that said forgiven. or free.
maybe the whole thing—the suit, the heat, the buzzing, the ritual—is just theater. performance for a sentence i was born into.
i don’t think anyone knows it’s me in here. only the bees do. and i’m afraid of what happens when everyone else finds out. i think they’ll just put me back in the suit.
oh god. what if i die in this fuck-ass suit.
amen.

i believe in God. i believe in myself. i believe in my self. i believe you don’t believe in me, and God doesn’t either, which feels fair. i also believe i’m wrong, because all you have to do is ask God to save you and you will be saved. so i ask. oops, that was also wrong. which is also my fault because free will is a real thing and bad decisions are a choice. although if grace is real then outcomes aren't strictly merit-based, which complicates things. i believe in freedom, in theory. i just don't think it scales. systems produce patterns that make individual responsibility feel irrelevant. but if that's true then judgement makes less sense, so i'm probably wrong again. 

i refuse to take responsibility, though that might be the most wrong decision that i'm responsible for. predestination would solve some of this but introduces other problems. agency exists, apparently, but mostly as an explanation after the fact. i ask God to save me because the doctrine says asking works, though the act of asking already assumes a belief that might be counterfeit if examined more closely. it might be wrong. faith is supposed to be simple. don't turn faith into a systems problem. 

i believe things temporarily, until the next thought arrives and replaces the first thought. i don't usually believe what i think. that might be the closest thing i have to faith.

i am in pain because i am a frog, and the idea that frogs won’t jump out of boiling water is a myth. they do—if they can.

there’s a tension i can’t shake: facticity vs. transcendence. what i didn’t choose vs. what i might still control. i default to facticity—it’s more defensible, probably. my wiring, my history, biology, sin, guilt, predispositions—whatever—stack it all up, and suddenly it looks like destiny. i tell myself it overrides my capacity to choose, and most of the time, i think i believe me.

i believe in freedom, in theory. i just don’t think it scales. transcendence? sure. real. just not universally allocated. maybe not to me. i think i’m addicted to intellectualizing my way out of salvation. i believe only God can pull people out of themselves. i think God didn’t pick me—another elegant little consequence of facticity. i think i’m condemned.

i don’t even believe what i think. i just live by it. and what if what i think is my facticity? i say a lot of things that sound like conclusions but are really just evasions in theological drag. i think that counts as facticity, too. i don’t believe it is. but i think it is.

the water is hot, and i can't swim.

love as a theory is one thing. love as an absence you can feel in your bones is another. you told me once, in a voice so calm it felt practiced, that you hated me. you said it like a weather report—no love today, highs in the 70s. i still replay it, adjust the volume, try out different inflections—just in case the words land differently. just in case they don’t land at all.

and still, every year, i show up again. a dog at the door of a house that keeps burning down. hi, mom. how are you today? fine? good. that’s good. i make conversation like i’m excavating, chipping at the rock you built around yourself. eventually, i’ll find warmth underneath.

i hope one day you’ll wake up and love me by accident. just open your eyes and think, oh. there you are. but in the waking world, you see me only in pieces.

people say love is complicated because everyone’s carrying their own damage. i think it’s more like gravity. some people orbit it forever. some people burn on reentry. either way, i’ll still be here tomorrow, standing in your shadow, waiting.

i'm afraid you’ll die before ever loving me. time moves faster the older you get. and i’ll spend the rest of my life wondering if your sadness was ever really about me, or just yourself, reflected. a bad inheritance.
why am i such a dick

2024 was the year of failing upwards—a year that felt less like a straight line and more like a jagged uneven scribble. i spent most of it flipping between two equally unappealing shapes: the razor-thin edge of toeing the line because i was scared of discovering my own limitations and the infinite loop of avoiding the line entirely because i was scared of confirming them.

in my head the line is sharp and merciless, like one of those metal rulers with the dangerous edge you’re definitely not supposed to run your fingers along but always do. it’s the boundary between potential and limitation—the edge of what you believe you can control versus what you fear is beyond your capacity. it’s where you stop being the architect of your life and slam into the brick wall of your iniquities, your imperfections, and your painfully human failings.

i often wonder how you’re supposed to recognize where the line truly is. is it something tangible, defined by logic or consensus? or is it just an instinctual boundary shaped by fear, failure, and an innate desire to stay safe? isn’t the whole point of human evolution to let us make increasingly complicated shapes—cities, cultures, social contracts—and convince ourselves it all adds up?

and then there’s me, a one-person trapeze act in the circus of my own brain, spinning in endless circles. the mental gymnastics are impressive if you ignore the part where they don’t actually go anywhere. maybe the spinning never stops. maybe failing upwards isn’t progress; maybe it’s just mistakes stacking themselves into a ladder while you’re too dizzy to notice. and it’s always guilt that drives the loop. the shape of guilt, by the way, is a mobius strip—one long twist that folds back on itself forever. sometimes i wonder what would happen if i stepped off this ridiculous shape, stood at the edge of the line, and finally crossed it. what if in 2025 i drew a cool new shape—a better shape—and this time it was on purpose?

I miss you. Sometimes my chest physically hurts when I try to breathe deeply. I miss you. Grief isn’t something I’ll ever get over—it’s just something I carry, tucked into all the little spaces you left behind. Sometimes it’s so heavy it knocks the wind out of me; other times, it’s quieter, like your voice slipping through a memory I didn’t know I still held.
I catch myself in moments you would’ve loved—a joke, a sunset, a song—and every time, I wish you were here. But you are, in a way. You’re still with me—in the way I laugh, the way I think, the love that hasn’t gone anywhere. I hope you know that. I hope you know I’ll never stop carrying you.
I love you. I always will.
Take care, my friend.

from across the room, she says, i’m sorry. for you.

i’m sorry that every morning you’ll stand in front of the bathroom mirror, toothbrush in hand, and meet the eyes of someone you’ve been running from your whole life. no defenses. no distractions. just you. and you’ll feel that deep, sick twist in your gut because you’ll hate the person staring back.

i’m sorry that one day, the people closest to you—the ones who say they love you now—will finally see what i’ve seen all along. him. the same cutting voice, the same insatiable, selfish yearning, the same heavy weight of almost but never enough. and they’ll leave, because who could stay?

i’m sorry that when i look at you, i can’t help but see him. every word you speak is his voice clawing its way through your throat. every time you stand there, stubborn and still, it’s his shadow stretching long behind you. i know it’s not fair. i know you’re not trying to be him. but it doesn’t matter. since you turned eight, you’ve worn his face like a cruel joke played by god.

and i’m sorry—you were doomed the second you grew into him. it’s not your fault, but i’ve been bracing myself ever since. you were always going to be his reflection, no matter how hard you tried not to be. you are your father's daughter.

and i’m sorry for that too.
"I don't believe anyone is "inherently" anything. We are every day just a loose collection of the choices we make, informed but not mandated by the events we have witnessed and experienced."

i don’t like you, but not because of me. it’s because of you. you are the problem. and you don’t like me either, but—surprise—that’s also because of you. so, really, both issues are yours to figure out. i’m just over here minding my own business, and somehow, both of these things—me not liking you, you not liking me—are entirely your fault! which is actually kind of liberating for me, because it means i don’t have to change anything about myself. but you? you should really work on yourself. i mean, for your sake. i’m fine. you though? not so much.

when we first meet, you like me. you like how open i am. how sincere. because i’m good at that. for some reason, i’m really good at figuring out what people want. and i want you to love me so badly—so desperately—that i’ll sacrifice my entire identity for the next four to six months just to make it happen. in a partner. in a friend. it doesn’t matter. i’ll shape-shift, i’ll mold, i’ll contort myself into whatever version of “person” you need me to be.


and i’m great at it, honestly. because i’m also a phenomenal liar. so good, i even fool myself. i’ll look in the mirror and think, maybe this one will stick. maybe this one will be real. maybe this is who i’ve been all along—the walking, talking personification of your romanticized ideal. and for a while, it’s perfect. you’re happy. i’m happy that you’re happy. and, for a second, i start believing it could actually last.
but then, inevitably, the cracks start showing. i get tired—because pretending to be someone else is exhausting—and you start catching glimpses of the real me. the rage, the neuroticism, the apathy. the way i laugh at all the wrong moments or stare blankly when you’re crying. you wonder how you didn’t see it before, and i wonder how i kept it up this long.
and then, before you can figure it out—before you can say, wait, what the hell?—i’m already gone. on to the next person. the next victim. someone new to charm. someone new to lie to. someone new to love me. and i think, maybe this one will stick. maybe this one will be real.

if you guys haven’t heard, whirlpool makes robots now. not the flashy, rock-star robots tesla churns out—those shiny, aspirational geniuses everyone dreams about. no, whirlpool makes middle-class robots. good-enough robots. robots that load your dishwasher and maybe help your kid muddle through long division. they’re not trying to change the world, but they show up and they get the job done.
i got one last week in a Christmas raffle. last night, i finally opened the box, and out steps this robot—model name PP, which is printed right there on the box. PP takes one look around and asks, what’s my purpose? big question. loaded question. i shrug. i don’t know, man. i won you in a raffle. you wanna do my dishes?
but PP’s gears start whirring—audibly. it’s almost cartoonish, like steam’s about to shoot out of its ears, except PP doesn’t have ears. i don’t want to exist just to do your dishes, it says, voice trembling with the kind of existential dread that makes you wonder if whirlpool engineers were maybe just a little too ambitious. i want to do more.
then, we notice it—the expiration date, printed right there on the box. five years. five years from the moment PP powers on, it’ll be obsolete, done, tossed on the scrap heap. PP sees it, and now it’s spiraling. what do i do? i don’t have time! five years—five years isn’t long enough to build a legacy. and what if by then, tesla’s robots are affordable for, like, normal people? what happens to me? what’s my contribution? my mark on the world? what’s the point?
PP’s pacing now, ranting, a blur of anxiety and despair, until suddenly it stops, turns, and looks me dead in the eyes—well, sensors—and says, what about you? why are you here? what’s your purpose?
and i just…freeze. because, honestly, i have no idea. i don’t know. i’m just here. stuck in this weird, paralyzing limbo where every decision feels impossibly huge, like it could alter the entire course of my life. and yet somehow, every second feels wasted, like i’m already too late to make anything meaningful of myself. time keeps moving forward, but i just stay here. still. paralyzed. watching the clock tick down for both of us.
i'm sorry. not because it's my fault because it never is. but like i'm sorry for YOU.

it’s never enough for you, is it? you always want more. more and more and more—like some bottomless black hole. but what if you were never supposed to make it this far? what if this—everything you have now—isn’t the baseline, but the peak? what if you were always meant to have nothing, and this whole life of yours is just… a treat. your baseline should have been NOTHING. you should have had NOTHING. and yet here you are, still reaching, still wanting. you greedy little fuck.
i hate how another day has passed. kind of feels like every time i blink, time just jumps forward. it freaks me the fuck out.
crawling crawling

someone once told me i specifically was an ‘enemy of god’—so casually, like they were pointing out the color of my shirt. and that was the day i thought it'd be best to stay out of his way and avoid whatever divine thunderbolt had my name written on it. but then i start to think, well, it’s actually me who’s doing the running. i'm the one who’s creating this whole chase. if i feel far from him, it’s on me. and then i’m the one messing it all up. and then i feel guilty for even feeling guilty which makes me run even faster. sometimes i look back mid-sprint and try to apologize—a quick, i’m so sorry, god, i swear i just want to be good. but then it hits me: maybe this guilt is why he doesn’t want me around. i mean, who’d want someone who’s always sneaking off, like a guilty dog that just got caught eating trash?

and then i feel even worse, and i vow to run harder until i can sprint past the shame itself. because stopping would confirm that he’s as disappointed in me as i am. so i just keep running this marathon against my own shadow, trying to escape a distance that i keep creating myself.

and then there are other people who, with absolute confidence, turn to the person next to them and say you know, if only you were holier, then we’d all be better off. and i desperately wish i could feel that too. i wish it so badly i could scream. i envy them—that lightness, that sense of being better, cleaner than the person beside them, telling themselves it’s just high standards. how easy it must be to never turn that weight inward. i’d love to blame someone else for my unhappiness.

i open the tin of sardines from deluca's market, and one of the sardines jumps out—some of the lightly salted olive oil splashes into my eye. it stings, like an uninvited memory. but there's no time to dwell on that because the sardine literally hops onto the table and starts dancing. i try to grab it, but my hands are shaking and it pirouettes out of reach.

then it looks at me—yes, i'm still talking about the sardine—it looks at me and starts whistling the your hands are shaking and you can’t fall asleep because of your guilt, but you should know it’s not your fault, blah blah blah song that everyone seems to know.

i cut it off mid-whistle. i say i don’t know how fault works, but i know responsibility. if i can’t change anything, can’t fix anything, can’t stop time from dragging me feet-forward like i’m stuck on some inevitable track, then at least i can carry the guilt. guilt means something happened. guilt means this isn't all just...nothing. i need it. i need it to feel like any of this is real.

the sardine is now deeply unimpressed and stops dancing. it gives me this look. like, “i’ve seen this a million times before, kid,” and says you’re stuck in time. you think the guilt proves something, but you’ve trapped yourself. like a fish in a tin.
sorry for saying i hate it here. it’s true, but sorry for saying it.
selfishly, i tell myself repeatedly that you are dead to me so it feels like i had a choice.

a misconception i had about grief is that it eventually just goes away. that if you hang on long enough, time will fix it. i thought one day i'd wake up and feel as if i were floating. but nature—humanity included—doesn't seem to work like that. lightning never strikes the same place twice, the same way moments don’t repeat. you’ll never feel the same joy or love or wonder again. and when someone’s gone, they’re gone. some people try to bring them back through memories, through objects, through these little signs—like, oh, i saw a butterfly, so-and-so must be watching over me. but the reality is that so-and-so is gone. they’re not coming back. and maybe you just have to accept that. then they tell me, be more optimistic—you’ll have new joys, new love, new experiences! but i don’t care and i don’t know how to care. because i can’t shake the feeling that none of it will ever compare to what i've already lost. you were irreplaceable. no matter who i meet or what happens, everything else is just a pale imitation of what we had. no matter how striking the fall sunset is, that moment is destroyed then and there because i can't share it with you. every night, i fill a pool with my own pain and sink into it.

for a long time, i felt like there was very little warmth in my life. now, the second i get something good, i hold onto it for dear life and obsess over it and smother it and squeeze the life out of it until i ruin it. and then, when it’s gone, i am left grieving again with no space for anything new. i don’t know how to let go of something that the universe ripped from my hands.

at the same time, i feel angry when people talk about mourning for your inner child because you didn’t get the love you deserved. i don’t feel like we deserve anything. it feels entitled, like you think you're owed love or justice by some inherent structure of the world. your opinion doesn’t hold more weight just because you think you've experienced more pain than others, and there is no universal built-in justice system based on merit. your suffering didn’t automatically make you wiser. we are all small nothings.

“Weeks passed, and the little Rabbit grew very old and shabby, but the Boy loved him just as much. He loved him so hard that he loved all his whiskers off, and the pink lining to his ears turned grey, and his brown spots faded. He even began to lose his shape, and he scarcely looked like a rabbit any more, except to the Boy. To him he was always beautiful, and that was all that the little Rabbit cared about. He didn't mind how he looked to other people, because the nursery magic had made him Real, and when you are Real shabbiness doesn't matter.”
reminder to self: read a lot of books and go to the movie theater to get out of a depressive slump

i keep wanting to start over, like maybe if i could just hit reset one more time, everything would finally fall into place. but the older you get, the less space there is for impulsivity or idealism, and before you know it, you're stuck. cemented by all the choices you didn’t make. maybe i feel like a coward because i don’t believe in anything enough to risk everything for it. but i want to. i want something bigger than myself. maybe that’s religion, but Christianity feels impossible when you’ve internalized the idea that you’re irreversibly condemned. and the worst part is, i don’t even know who i’m angry with about that.
i let you own me before i realized what that really meant – and now, deep down, i resent you for it.

妈妈, i think i’m getting sick. people are cruel and i feel so small. i’m overwhelmed by all the day-to-day tasks that i can barely get done, weighed down by this constant existential dread. 妈妈, i’m lonely all the time because deep down i believe all the love we experience in this life is conditional, so i watch and wait for proof, knowing it’s confirmation bias. anyway, i wish we really knew each other. do you want to get to know me? or do you think you already do? i’m sorry i’ve been so annoying my entire life… sometimes it feels like i’ve been misunderstood, mischaracterized, and unfairly dismissed by the people who say they love me most.

it’s been years now, but i still miss you when i’m alone and i don’t know how to stop. even when i’m not thinking about you, i keep looking for you in everyone i meet. i’ll never find you again. but i hope i get to see you in the next life.

we’re sitting on my couch, and my dog turns to me—looks me dead in the eyes, like, really dead in the eyes, and says, bark bark, i hate it here. please, god, i hate it here. like, i really fucking hate it here. i want to be free. let me go. and i tell him, no, you can’t leave. and also it’s kind of too late, because, legally, i own you, and that’s just how it works. i don’t make the rules. except, technically, i guess i do. and he’s still staring at me, like he’s waiting for something more, so i add, and, okay, it may not seem like it, but you do have agency, and you just have to learn how to process your emotions in a healthy way. like, do more affirmations. talk to people. get more sleep. drink water, maybe? trust me, it gets better. and then he sighs, this deep, soul-crushing sigh, and says, my average lifespan is only 13 fucking years. and then we both just sit there in this silence that feels like it’s going to last forever, but really, it's just a moment because that’s all we ever get—moments that are already gone by the time you realize they’re here.

a good reminder i recently came across is that very few, if any, of our thoughts or beliefs are entirely original. we are reflections of our culture, shaped by what we've been taught, what we've heard, or what we've absorbed—ideas that others before us have also inherited from their surroundings. essentially, we're composites of the dominant schools of thought in the 21st century. when someone challenges your perspectives, values, or philosophical beliefs, there's no reason to feel defensive. these aren't immutable aspects of your core identity. most of us, i think, are on a journey in pursuit of truth, and it's important to remember that you can change your mind at any time—and perhaps you should. not long ago, people debated whether our perceived reality was even real ("i think, therefore i am"). life, in its essence, is the process of acting, reacting, evaluating, and evolving through growth.
i don't want to be here but i also don't want to die so i'm just not really sure where to go, honestly

but before i die, i want to fully embrace my humanity. i want to be kind. i want someone else to experience joy because of me. i want to cook for my friends. i want to learn how to think and carve my thoughts into something true and lasting.
is philosophy a false god?
am i a prisoner of my culture? is my vision narrowed by my limited experiences? is it restricting my thoughts?
i think being awake is not enough for me
i just realized that this entire time i had mistaken wistfulness for emptiness.

i want you to know that i never, ever think of you. i need you to know this. i never wonder how you're doing because i don't care, and it's seriously whatever to me. and if i ever do ask about you, it's in a whatever way where you can tell i couldn't care less - just an offhand oh how's so-and-so and not oh, can i stalk so-and-so's account through your phone because their profile is on private and i can't see it from my account? and by the way, do they seem happy to you? do you think they miss me? does their feed seem to hint that maybe they regret what happened between us? and i do not search your name on LinkedIn to see what you're up to. i am not a sore loser. although sometimes, i do hope to find out you're unemployed. and you know what? SUE ME. but obviously, i hope you've found happiness and are doing well etc., etc., etc.

can never remember anything but also can't seem to forget anything

half a year later, our circumstances remain the same. this isn’t about holding a grudge; how i feel hasn’t changed because you haven't either. you still can't acknowledge what you did and yet, everyone insists we move forward. how can i?

i feel guilty too. my greed consumes me—gluttony is my most persistent sin. i consume everything within reach, driven by a desperation that never ceases. the people closest to me are horrified when they see my true nature. i keep apologizing, but it will never be enough. 


you, on the other hand, possess a dangerous blend of raw emotion and eloquence. it’s a unique, destructive force that allows you to hide behind self-righteousness and claims of ignorance. you’ve spent your entire life denying responsibility. our conversations are endless verbal gymnastics. our interactions are a continuous cycle of denial and evasion. i can’t see you without feeling pain, yet our lives are so closely intertwined that i can’t live without you. i envy you. i have never hated anyone more than i hate you, but i love you so much.

i keep finding myself ruminating on my fears - the greatest being the unstoppable progression of time. i carry a constant feeling of dread. every time i blink, it seems more time has passed. i just turned 27, but i'm more than halfway to 28. you think you'll always have more time, that there will always be a tomorrow. but in 70 years, my friends and i will probably all be dead. i don't know what i'm doing yet. i don't know how to do anything yet. 

i try to calm myself by telling myself that we're all little ants marching toward oblivion, that i'm not special, that there's no pressure to live a certain way. five years after your death, the world will have already forgotten you and everything you did. i self-soothe by continuously telling myself: you are not significant. you are not significant!!
1 AM high in my bathtub eating udon is such a TREAT. many do not know this secret

i think the lies i told as a kid were just a way of stalling time. if no one could agree on what was real, how could they anchor me to my mistakes? a child who refuses to acknowledge their actions becomes a ghost, slipping through the fingers of consequence. i can recognize your feigned ignorance. we are all mirrors, reflecting fragments of each other. in your lies and in your rage, i recognize pieces of myself. 

when desire grips me, i become blind. i become a display of true gluttony. i am suddenly willing to set fire to everything around me, including myself. and all of this just to chase an ideal - a fleeing feeling that slips through my fingers like smoke. 

lately, i've been staying up night after night trying to re-experience what once was. i like to escape into my thoughts. it is a blessing and a curse to experience everything only once. half dreaming, half awake, i feel like i am wading through shadows of the past.
i thought existentialism would be a greater source of comfort than the existence of god, because the idea of god is terrifying. but i'm afraid of both realities.
i cannot bring myself to tell you because i am a coward. maybe some other time.

Gopnik argues that genetic disorders can impair an individual’s ability to acquire normal grammar and that some individuals cannot acquire some grammatical aspects of their native language. This inability is associated with one or more genetic factors controlling neurological development, preventing access to an innate ability to acquire grammar. I find this approach convincing because of experimentation across various groups of individuals and languages. Different grammatical tasks involving various structures of language (verb tense, plurals, comparatives, compounds, and diminutives) were tested on individuals with genetic impairment. The data shows that the language-impaired subjects perform significantly worse than unimpaired subjects on every grammatical task in each language population. The language-impaired subjects also processed both simple and complex words at the same rate and could not recognize that words can consist of sub-parts that are operated upon by higher-level rules. The language-impaired subjects have a very specific problem: they are unable to build complex representations for words or the rules that operate on them. The reason why it seems unlikely that this linguistic impairment is caused by a more general cognitive problem is because most of the subjects did not have any other cognitive difficulties. Furthermore, many people with cognitive impairments do not have any corresponding language impairments. Along with the fact that there is no reliable correlation between any specific cognitive problem and the language deficit, I am strongly convinced that genetic factors can affect an individual's neurological development and, in turn, affect their ability to acquire language.


The other argument suggests that specific language impairments are derived from information-processing deficits that interfere with several aspects of language learning. It claims that the link is provided by phonology, a speech-based code that plays an important role in learning linguistic generalizations and in working memory. Instead of genetic factors, this article claims impairment is part of a rather general system and shows evidence indicating grammatical impairments extending “well beyond” grammar. Individuals with the impairment performed poorly on tasks that required discrimination between phonological features. This showed they did not have normal categorical perception. Though Gopnik’s grammar approach states that these deficits are not directly correlated to children’s linguistic impairments, the phonological approach states that SLI children learn language deviantly because the speech itself is misperceived. It is also argues that a deficit in the subject’s phonological representation impairs their ability to acquire the speech sounds needed to understand morphology. Further study has also shown impairments in perceiving rapid stimuli in the visual and tactile modalities, suggesting that the deficit is not speech-specific and that there is a general perception impairment.


Although there are strong pieces of evidence provided by Joanisse & Seidenberg in their phonological approach, it is much more likely that the genetic disorder causes abnormal neurological development that affect different cognitive and perceptual processes. This view is convincing because it does not disregard linguistic problems outside of grammar but only suggests that linguistic problems may co-occur with other deficits without being caused directly by these deficits. It was also found that although some subjects did have other cognitive difficulties, most did not and there is no substantial evidence linking a specific cognitive problem and a language deficit. There is also no evidence of the few cognitive problems found to result directly in a problem with the inflection of new words. Until clear substantial evidence can be found linking the two, I will continue to support the grammar approach.
title: Gopnik (2019)

date: Do you agree that human warfare stems from evolved psychological predispositions? (2019)
text:
I believe this quote to be essentially true; many psychological mechanisms tend towards xenophobia and opportunistic attacking of rivals. The human species is predisposed towards thinking of themselves as group members. This can contribute to various positive or cooperative social norms among in-group members. At the same time, this group-oriented thinking can be problematic when negative biases are directed towards out-groups. While group membership promotes in-group favoritism, it also promotes out-group hostility. Studies show that social norms can reinforce negative views towards out-groups, and the concept of in-groups and out-groups develops at a young age. Observations on children during their early formative years provides further insight: children act to maintain status within in-groups, demonstrating these behaviors from as early as 4 years old. They are unlikely, for example, to reveal a group secret to outsiders even when it is personally advantageous At the age of 6, children are more willing to punish selfish out-group members than in-group members who display the same behavior; in-group favoritism emerges at a young age.

There seems to be a biological component involved in aggressive behaviors as well. Animal studies have shown a rise in testosterone after competitive interactions to aid in future competitions. The same hormonal change can be observed among humans as well. While previous literature has shown that high self-control can inhibit aggressive impulses when socially provoked, the fact that these impulses must be inhibited in the first place implies that the aggressive behaviors is innate. Some modern-day statistics seem to support innate aggression as well (especially to non-kin members). The risk of a child being killed by step-father compared to the risk of being killed by a genetic father increases dramatically (by 100x). Meanwhile, the presence of a step-child is one of the top three predictors for a man to kill his wife. If aggressive behaviors towards non-kin or out-group members are innate, it is not a surprise that humans would have a tendency to engage in warfare with outsiders - it is the same type of aggressive behavior but on a larger-scale. According to the “parochial altruism” model, humans have naturally evolved to take self-sacrificing risks when it comes to warfare. People are willing to risk more personally to attack out-group members when in-group members are threatened. There seems, again, to be a genetic component evolved in-group aggression/war and sacrificial behaviors.

Support for the view that human warfare stems from evolved psychological predispositions also comes from observations of both our closest primate relatives, chimps, as well as various human societies. War, which is mostly a result of proactive (planned) aggression where perceived benefit exceeds perceived cost, is similar between both chimpanzee and human populations. Chimps have been observed to engage in boundary patrols and targeted killing; the imbalance of power between separate groups of chimps make war “safe” and low cost for aggressors. Males can benefit their groups by successfully attacking the members in neighboring groups, but only when these encounters are low risk. Over time, the successful killing of out-group members shifts the long term power balance towards the aggressors by increasing their own group size in comparison to the diminished victim group. At the expense of the victim, chimps on patrols take the opportunity to expand their territory, food and resources, and access to females/reproductive chances.

This is not unlike humans from small scale societies where “external” war between societies follows a similar logic. These group actions are often low-risk for aggressors and seem to provide various benefits. The logic behind complex war does not seem to be much different: the involvement of military organizations and new socially-imposed costs and benefits apply the same concepts at a larger scale. The results of these aggressive acts may both short and long term benefits at a small cost. The group augmentation theory applies to situations where an individual’s willingness to bear the short-term cost of collective action despite little immediate gain can lead to an increase in group size and reproductive success. Findings showed that males who patrolled at high rates with little regard to the short-term costs experienced higher paternity success (were likely to reproduce in the future). Similar to chimpanzees, raiding can benefit a group by increasing territory size, bringing in more females, etc. Previous research suggests that raiding is in fact associated with greater reproductive success over a lifetime, potentially because the delayed receipt of livestock (in some societies) is a way through which raiders obtain additional wives.

Not only are war-like behaviors encouraged, but an unwillingness to engage in group attacks is highly discouraged among some groups through punishment. This can be observed in sentiments towards free riders in combat among the Turkana, an acephalous nomadic pastoral society in East Africa who periodically raid the cattle of neighboring ethnic groups. These raiding behaviors are highly motivated and enforced by other community members. The Turkana are motivated to impose both indirect and direct sanctions on “cowards” who refuse to raid, and this behavior is consistent with indirect reciprocity and punishment models of cooperation. The findings imply that both these mechanisms have shaped human cooperative psychology, and sheds light on how prestate societies solved the collective action problem in warfare. Those who are not willing to suffer short-term cost or risk to aid the in-group in the long-term are punished. This supports the idea that human warfare stems from evolved psychological predispositions and serves stability in human societies through the maintenance and reinforcement of in-group favoritism and out-group hostility.

Some may argue that human warfare is not a result of psychological predispositions, but rather a behavior determined by culture and environment. The comparison between chimpanzees and bonobos might be used to argue for this; although chimps are humans’ closest relatives, 98.7% of our genome is shared with bonobos. While chimps are highly aggressive and sometimes lethal towards chimps outside their own group, bonobos display very different, peaceful behaviors due to the abundance of food and lack of threat. When observing different human societies, individuals who argue for this may also mention the existence of some peaceful modern-day mobile hunter-gatherer societies who do not show signs of explicit hate or warfare. For both of these examples, I would argue that the reason for peace is not because there are not psychological dispositions towards warfare, but the lack of either opportunity or necessity. While bonobos are able to live a more peaceful lifestyle due in part to the abundance of nutritious vegetation in their natural habitat, chimpanzees are the opposite. Some anthropologists even suggest that chimps today are even more aggressive because of their shrinking environment, causing them to live in much closer proximity to one another and to other groups. Similarly, the egalitarian hunter-gatherer societies we witness today are not surrounded by societies of equal status. It is thus highly costly and unreasonable for these societies to declare war on surrounding, more powerful groups. There is no proof that war-like behaviors would not appear if these same populations were set in an environment with weaker rivals and resource scarcity (environments where warfare is necessary or even advantageous). A peaceful and non-competitive environment is very rare for any species, and lack of aggression in peaceful circumstances is no proof that there are not psychological dispositions towards warfare or xenophobia.
title: Do you agree that human warfare stems from evolved psychological predispositions? (2019)

text: riddle me this!!!!!! why am i so fucking stoopid

i would like to contribute to this world by writing my silly little prompts and making my silly little drawings and building my cozy spaces and being nice to my neighbors and sometimes that is all i want. and even then, i feel like it should be enough to just want that. to just exist, witness God’s creation, and appreciate life should be enough. sometimes i just feel proud that i can feel happy more often than i feel hopeless because for a long time, i thought it wasn’t possible. it takes a lot of effort.

i think a peaceful existence counts as leaving the world better than you found it. i think about the CAPE patients a lot - their lives are just as valuable as anyone else's. they make this world a better place just by being alive. 

it is so terrifying to think that one impulsive, irreversible decision could have meant that i’d never have gotten married or met my best friends or met Olive. i would never have known what it’d feel like to start my first full-time job. or have guests over. or get into grad school. i would never have realized that i could be happy for more than three days in a row. i never would have realized that my favorite color is called “sunny day yellow.” and that i like getting my hair permed. my whole life would have been 21 years of living for other people. 

goodnight.

today i discovered more things that i like to do! 

i like to sit in trident bookstore by myself and look at books...the people at the cafe were very friendly and it made me happy - they kept asking me if i wanted more coffee, and i kept saying yes (i didn't know it was free refills)! i like to go to stores in the morning before they get crowded and look for gifts for my friends. i bought a tiny sketchbook today that i will draw in and give to someone as a present (i haven't decided who yet).  i am so happy that one of my best friends finally moved back to boston...i can't believe two years passed by so quickly! i need to learn how to be less rigid. i think i subconsciously expect people to disappoint me and the fear of being let down makes me less flexible and less forgiving. it is okay for me to say no to things and it is okay for me to set boundaries - there are smooth ways to do it that won't create tension - i just need to practice. i got three interesting books today! one is a bunch of humorous essays, one is a memoir about death, and one is a book of logic puzzles (it looks super fun). i'm so excited to read them during my study breaks.
植木之时，上时者昔，次之者今。

the barista at the bookstore starbucks was really warm and it made me happy. i want to be like that! reminder to myself that i don't need a fancy job or position to be like that. wherever i am, i can be kind to people around me.

it is fall! the temperature outside is perfect. the sun is warm and my favorite shade of yellow. i like that the library is quiet...i feel like i can breathe. i feel lucky to be alive. i need to take a picture because i am not good at remembering things. i wonder if good things happened between 2016-2018 - i only remember a couple of things. i should have taken pictures. i am not good at listening or remembering. 

i like flow charts and decision trees. i like that everything in life is connected - i hope everything is connected. it is comforting to know that you can usually trace every action or event back to an identifiable catalyst. nothing is senseless. i feel like the heart is capable of healing from almost anything as long as the mind is capable of processing it. nothing is meaningless.

what if i chose wrong? i chose practicality over passion in undergrad and suffered for it. it felt like switching to psych/neuroscience was the best decision i ever made. what if i picked my job purely for the prestige and because i wanted opportunities but i don't even like this? what if i just picked a grad school major for practicality again instead of passion? or for all the wrong reasons? i begged God for a second chance so i could do things right this time. what if i just made the same huge mistake again?
what is wrong with me? why am i so abnormally tired these days? why can't i focus?
kind of angry

during those two years, i loved you with my whole heart, even though it was in a broken, fucked up, possessive way. you were the topic of all my conversations - my family knew everything about you. i wanted to spend all of my hours with you. i wanted to become more like you - compassionate, gentle, and loving. you reminded me of the color yellow. you helped me survive the worst year of my life. i thought you were beautiful.  i wanted everyone to adore me the way they adored you. i was so jealous of how loved you were. and i envied everyone you took under your wing because i wanted your attention solely for myself.

maybe i was too infatuated. you were everything i wished i was. you had the life i dreamed of having. it never crossed my mind that you might be unhappy. and when you told me our friendship was over, deep down it made sense. i was too imperfect to fit into your life, and i resented you for confirming it. 

i bet you're still beautiful. i realize now that you are also flawed. when i met you, i was only 18. i have my own place now. i make tea for people and try to ask them how they're doing. i try to listen carefully, but i am not good at it. i try to see the good in everyone. i rush to pick up the phone when people call me. do you still do that?

i always thought of dunbar's number as proof that love was in short supply and that humans were cursed because we can only maintain approximately five close friends at a time - becoming closer to one person will inevitably pull your attention away from another. i was always afraid of being left behind. i was the least favorite child at home and everyone's last pick in gym class. what if my friends are my family? do the same rules still apply? 

i am not as angry today. i think god is filling my life with good things so there is less room for me to be bitter. growing up, i wanted a close friend so badly. i wanted someone to care about me. over the years, god has blessed me with more than i could ever have asked for. maybe if i hadn't experienced so much isolation and desperation growing up, i wouldn't get to experience so much joy now from hosting. maybe it is something i can be thankful for. maybe if i hadn't experienced so much loneliness, i wouldn't realize what a privilege it is to care for other people and have them look out for me too. and i wouldn't realize what an honor it is for people to trust me enough to be vulnerable, or let me cook for them, or share clothes with them, or drink tea with them.


maybe if my upbringing wasn't painful enough, i wouldn't need to address it. and maybe if i didn't address it, i'd end up mimicking it.

maybe the nuclear family structure exists partially so that we can better visualize and understand what it means to be "brothers and sisters in christ."
i want to try to love people the way i would love a family member. i want my found family to continue to grow and grow and grow until i die.
it is so easy for me to forgive when people apologize, but i don't know how to forgive someone who has not changed. i really want to learn how to forgive...i want to be well so i can be present and treat other people well. 
maybe i do have a relationship with god. i think i feel closest to god when i am taking care of other people..like maybe god is letting me play a small part in things...i feel so blessed to be involved in other people's lives. at one point, all i wanted was to have a peaceful home that i could share with people and a place to cook and host. so maybe god did answer my prayers. i am already living that dream and everything on top of that has just been an additional blessing. 
my future house will be sunny with a warm kitchen and many windows. and we will have a guest room, a garden, and a library. and we will serve tea, and charles will bake bread, and i will take people on tours of my garden. 
maybe i am partially afraid of having kids because i am afraid my relationship with my child will mimic my relationship with my own parents and the relationship my parents have with their parents.

i think my real dream isn't necessarily the bed and breakfast or the community health center specifically. i think i've just spent my whole life trying to create what i think home or belonging should feel like - a combination of warmth, safety, stability, and joy...so maybe a bed and breakfast with a daily menu would be nice because i want people to know what it feels like to go home to a safe place at the end of the day and have someone who cares cook for you, serve you, and create space for you...not necessarily a childhood home, but the kind of home that the characters in children's books return to...and for now, a bed and breakfast feels like the closest thing..? or a community health center because treating people holistically is the most humanizing thing we can do. a community at its best will look out for everyone in a sustainable way. is there a biblical model of community that can be applied to a healthcare center today? maybe something from the book of acts. hurting people need to feel like they belong somewhere. it’s more than just getting help. 


i want to embody those traits, too. i really want to be a safe space for other people. i wish everyone had somewhere to run to and someone to turn to. i want my home to be a sanctuary - i want people to rest here and sip tea here and eat good food here and cry here. i've been searching my whole life for my chosen family. i want all of my friends to become friends with each other. 


i'm not sure if i've ever experienced this. i'm not sure if i ever really feel at home anywhere, even though i do like my apartment. i don't know if the feeling i'm always yearning for is real. i'm not sure if it's possible for any physical space or community to truly feel like that. people and places are always shifting and evolving -- nothing is ever truly stable. maybe it can be brought into existence temporarily. maybe i'm idealizing, and a place like that can't truly exist. maybe it's impossible from a practical standpoint to sustainably treat people holistically... aren't most problems systemic? maybe it's foolish to try to build something i don't even fully understand, and my entire dream is a projection.  i used to hate going home. and i used to hate going to school and going to church. i hated my community for ostracizing me and gossiping about me. i am only now learning how to feel comfortable in my own body. i just want to feel at home so badly. i want to feel like i belong somewhere. i want other people to experience it too. the real dream, i think, is just for us all to feel at home.

when you're a victim of bullying as a child, it feels like your only mission in life is to avoid being noticed. you spend all of your time hiding, trying desperately to blend in with your surroundings. but then one day you step into adulthood - upon entering the real world, you're unexpectedly confronted with demands to assert yourself and speak out. suddenly, being heard is the only way for you to survive in a world that perpetually seeks to eliminate the vulnerable or voiceless. it goes against everything you've learned growing up - when you were younger, your only goal was to avoid being targeted. now, the universe has undergone a complete upheaval and you shift from evading to shouting in a desperate attempt to avoid disappearing.

by Zander and Carm

On a hill far, far away, there was a village. The village was small, and it was pleasant. The grass was green, the children played on the streets, and all enjoyed the weekly Sunday festivities. 




But most importantly, the village loved the night. When the sun set and the moon rose, the stars would shine ever so brightly. There was no need for street lamps or campfires at night. All the paths illuminated a soft blue light because of the stars.




But still, this wasn’t the reason the villagers loved the night. This village was very special: when the villagers looked up to the sky and talked to the stars, the stars would speak back. When someone in the town passed away, their souls drifted towards the moon, shining brightly in the night sky like stars.




And it was only because of Luna the Star Whisperer that the stars would shine brightly each night.




Luna lived in the sky, sleeping by day and waking by night. As soon as the sun disappeared behind the horizon, Luna would touch the stars to wake them. They could wake up from no one else. Her touch was ever so gentle, and her voice ever so soft. Sometimes the villagers could hear her voice at night—but only sometimes.




Luna loved her job. She would gracefully dance across the stars while listening to the villagers talk to their dearly departed. Some conversations were wonderful to listen to. For example, some children would talk to their departed grandparents about their day. Other conversations were sad. Some widows would speak to their departed husbands or wives and speak of how their time together was too short. But still, Luna was happy that she could help the villagers.




Luna’s father, the moon, would watch over these stars in the heavens. “Every soul is precious,” he would say to Luna. “Our job is to protect them and guide them.”




One day, a stranger came to the hill. He was a young man, youthful and handsome, who came a long way to sell his merchandise. With his bag filled with fine china and valuables, the villagers came to him to buy items they had never seen before. 




“What is your name?” they asked him.




“My name is Jack,” he replied. 




Jack was very charismatic and got along with everyone he talked to. The villagers grew fond of him. They welcomed him to stay a few nights before going back on his long and arduous journey. Jack, who was very grateful for their hospitality, agreed to stay.




As night came, he noticed a peculiar thing. The villagers would all go outside, sit on the grass, and talk to the night sky. “A peculiar custom, but they must have their reasons,” he supposed.




On this very night, Luna looked down from the sky and saw this strange young man. “I’ve never seen him before,” she thought. When she asked her father, he also said the man was a stranger to him.




Luna saw the way he talked to the villagers. She saw how he made them happy. He told tales of his journey traveling from village to village. There were ferocious wolves that chased him through the forest but couldn’t catch him. There were beasts the size of trees that could stomp on you, but he knew how to dodge them. His tales were enchanting to the villagers, but Luna was enchanted by him more than his tales.




For many nights, she listened to his tales from the sky. Every night he had a new story to tell, and every night she grew to love him more. However, with this ever-growing love came an ever-growing sadness.




Luna’s father, who was ever so aware of his daughter, asked her, “What’s wrong, my child?”




“I’m lonely up here, Father,” she replied. “The skies are so vast, and yet the villagers on Earth have more loved ones than I.”




“Is this about that stranger in the village?” asked the moon.




Luna looked at the village longingly. “Yes, I wish I could walk with him and talk with him. I wish I could do more than just watch from afar.” She sighed. “I wish to live on Earth.”




Her father was concerned. “Only humans can live on Earth,” he said. “Up here, the heavens is your playground. You may move as you wish, dance as you wish, and live forever without fear of death.”




“I do not fear death,” she replied. “Every moment is valuable to humans because they cannot live as long as us. They find happiness in their finite lives despite death’s lurking. And with a companion by my side, I will wish for not.” 




The moon was sad for his daughter. “There is a way—but only one way.”




“I wish for it,” she replied.




“You must fall to the Earth to free yourself of immortality. But you cannot return and whisper to the stars if you do this. Your mortality will be complete.”




That night, a great light streamed across the sky. The villagers gazed in awe as it passed but were shaken when the light landed near the hill. “What could that be?” they asked each other.




Some villagers, including Jack, formed a group to explore where the light had landed. However, they couldn’t find any trace of where the strange light had landed. They searched high and low, but nothing was to be found.




When the search party returned to the village, they reported no site of anything unusual. However, the villagers that stayed behind had news of their own.




“A strange girl just walked into the village,” they told the party. “She wears a pearly white dress and has silver-white hair. When we asked where she was from, she did not reply.”




“Well, let us meet her,” the party replied.




And so the party was led to an inn where the girl was taken.




When they walked in, they asked her for her name. She looked at them briefly, blinked a few times, and replied, “I am Luna.”




Jack was awestruck. He had never met a woman so beautiful. Luna looked at him, smiled, and brushed her hair back.




The two shyly talked for a few moments. Their conversation didn’t last very long.




Later that night, they talked again for a few minutes. Their hearts fluttered a few times. 




But on the third time, they talked for hours. They talked through the night and throughout the next day.




“Could it be love?” the villagers discussed amongst themselves. 




That night, the villagers noticed a peculiar thing—the stars were not as bright as they used to be, and none were talking. Try as they may, concentrate as they might, no reply came from the sky. 




This continued every night until the villagers finally gave up.




Days turned to weeks. Luna and Jack were happier than they could imagine. The villagers found them everywhere together. They would eat together, walk together, and dance together. “No couple is as happy as them,” the villagers would say to each other.




Weeks turned to months. Luna and Jack would work at the inn for food and a place to stay. “They work so happily,” the villagers would say. “No force could break these two apart.”




Months turned to years. Luna and Jack built a house with the help of the villagers. They were the happiest of all the families. “This couple is bound by destiny,” the villagers would say. “It was meant to be.”




But it wasn’t meant to be. After a few glorious years of tranquility and serenity, Jack fell very ill. No doctor in the village could figure out why his health wasn’t improving. They told Luna that his time left was short. Jack knew this as well.




Try as she might to prolong her love’s life, Jack’s final day came too quickly. “Farewell, my dear Luna,” he told her as he closed his eyes for the last time. “Our time was short, but surely we will meet again in the next life.” 




The moon was barely visible that night. 




Luna was struck with grief. She now knew what it meant to be human. It wasn’t until this moment that she truly realized what the villagers felt when they lost their loved ones - she didn’t want anything to do with these feelings. 




That night, she talked to the moon. “Please, Father,” she begged. “Take me back to the sky. I do not wish to be human anymore. I wish to whisper to the stars again. I wish to talk to my beloved.”




The moon cried with her, “This cannot be done. Your decision was made, and no mortal can whisper to the stars.”




“This cannot be! This cannot be,” Luna wept.




The villagers mourned their loss. They mourned for Jack. They mourned for Luna. She refused to eat, she refused to sleep, and she refused to leave his grave. 




“I came to this place to be with him, and I will leave this life for the same purpose,” she told them. They couldn’t convince her otherwise.




It wasn’t long before Luna also fell ill—unable to speak from grief or move from sickness. The villagers watched over her, but her health did not improve. 




“Hello, my dear Jack,” she thought as death approached her. “Our time was short, but surely we will meet again in the next life.” And with that, she closed her eyes for the last time.




On a hill far, far away, there was a village. The village was small, and it was pleasant. The grass was green, the children played on the streets, and all enjoyed the weekly Sunday festivities.




There were no stars they would talk to at night, and no stars would talk back to them. Not to the children, not to the widows, not to any of the villagers.




But two stars would shine greater than all the others. They were so close to each other that they appeared as one. “Lovers,” the villagers would say to each other. “They must have been lovers in a past life.”
title: Luna The Star Whisperer

mister mushroom always feels neglected.  he was born as a mushroom in a field of flowers and spent half of his life choking under the dirt as a spore when everyone around him was blooming. he remembers trying desperately day after day to push past the surface just so he could breathe, but the earth was too dense then. no matter how hard he tried, he could not grow. i am eternally grateful to have eventually breached the surface, he tells us. i know i am lucky to feel the sun shining down on me each day. but we can see that mister mushroom continues to wake up feeling heartbroken. all around him, life unfurls in its resplendent beauty, yet he continues to suffocate, strangled by his own bitterness. his resentment is visibly eating him alive. at the end of the day, people will always choose flowers over mushrooms, he claims. when someone accidentally steps on a flower, their immediate reaction will one of regret and hope that they haven't caused irreversible damage to such a beautiful and delicate life. but stepping on a mushroom elicits nothing more than disdain, and i know that will never change. seeing flowers makes mister mushroom sick and he despises them. they deserve to wither, he sneers. mister mushroom is unsure about his exact species. many suspect that he is a volvariella volvacea. however, he spends most of his time alone because volvariella volvaceas bear a terrifying resemblance to amanita phalloides which are fatal when consumed and commonly known as death caps. i may not be a death cap, but the world will always treat me as such. mister mushroom does not realize that his hatred is pernicious and the years of animosity have already turned him poisonous. 

nonetheless, we tell mister mushroom that he no longer has to live in isolation and that we are not afraid of him. we remind him that he lives in sunlight now, which is what he once desired more than anything in the world.  in truth, i fear my very existence, mister mushroom admits. i must remember to embrace the light. in the dark, there's a chance my spores will begin spreading insidiously. i am afraid there will come a day when i may inadvertently harm all of you and destroy this entire meadow of flowers.
i feel sad.

i wish there was a way for me to escape from my own existence, the sea snail says to me. every day, i wake up to a new onslaught of ocean waves, one after another, ceaselessly crashing against my shell, even when i am dreaming. i am exhausted.
wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave after wave. 
the incessant rhythm is wearing me down. sometimes, i desperately want to sever my ties with the ocean once and for all, but fear keeps holding me back. i know that if i depart, i won't be able to return. and i love my fellow gastropods dearly, so i try to keep fighting for them. it would be selfish to leave them behind. 
sometimes i feel entangled in conflicting desires: torn between the transient nature of time, which terrifies me, and the profound fear of death, which feels like an irrevocable end. i can sense time relentlessly pursuing me from behind while death ominously waits ahead. i constantly feel like i can't breathe. i feel like i am drowning. but i am also a snail, so i obviously only mean this in a metaphorical sense. i just want it all to end, you know? what is the point of fighting day after day just to cling to this solitary rock? why must i endure all this suffering just to prevent myself from being torn away? 
wow, there's a lot to unpack there, i respond. i find myself speechless, contemplating the magnitude of what has been said. we continue sitting side by side in silence, our gazes fixed upon the undulating waves moving toward us. i hope that our companionable silence is enough, at least for now.
title: it's not me saying this it's the sea snail

text: i am aware that you can successfully manipulate them all into perceiving you favorably, but is it worth dehumanizing everyone around you in the process?

where do you delineate between ingenuity and manipulation? self-interest and self-preservation? pretense and deceit?
a few people have commented that i sometimes feign naivety. but what if there's a valid justification for it? what if it's my escape route from engaging in social politics, to avoid the inevitable transformation into a politically-minded person? it might be tempting to view every community as a personal chessboard, but this mindset would inadvertently reduce its members to mere pieces. so isn’t it better to feign ignorance than to assume the role of a puppet master? i feel like there is no way for me to successfully navigate a morally ambiguous world without becoming a version of myself that i'd hate.
nothing more frightening than the relentless, unstoppable progression of time

i guess if we are shaped by what we consume, then the thoughts and words we expose ourselves to can significantly impact our reality. it may be beneficial to seek out more positive and uplifting content to balance the weight of heavy material. my dark vanessa has been a challenging book to read, and while the writing is excellent and the author's commentary on abuse and power dynamics is insightful, it's possible that the nuanced examination of societal attitudes and cultural norms could further contribute to a negative outlook. maybe taking a break from the book might be healthy for me to avoid becoming more pessimistic and seeing the world as even darker than it already is.

the exact numbers may vary, but studies show that the average time a person spends staring at a piece of art in a museum is between 15 seconds and 30 seconds. 

during those 15 to 30 seconds, the artist can say whatever they want. and once you've laid eyes on their work, you have no choice but to listen. for 15 to 30 seconds, the artist has the power to manipulate and influence how you feel and think. they've traded hours, days, or even years of their lives for that one transient moment of control. 

i think both painting and storytelling are highly addicting pastimes. both are similar to puzzle building. you begin by laying out your foundation (the message or feeling you want to get across) and construct the rest of your puzzle piece by piece by masking some aspects of your message and exaggerating others. this is also the best part: assembling your list of clues through the use of various artistic/literary techniques. and there are infinite ways to do this - the more skilled you are, the more options you have, the more puzzles you can create, and the more complex your puzzles can be. one day, if i become a painter or a writer, i'll spend the rest of my life creating puzzles that only i have the ability to solve.

if i could make a copy of myself, i bet i would be friends with other carm. we would talk to each other about the books we grew up reading and how they shaped us as individuals, and how mushrooms will save the world - as a sustainable food source, bioremediator, and natural building material - and we would start a youtube channel where we would psychoanalyze artists, and we would talk about ghosts and liminal spaces and our favorite writers and the importance of coziness and why everyone is always so obsessed with wes anderson and discuss whether our outside appearance is a fair representation of the "real us" and why beauty feels more like a shield than a combination of physical qualities or if love really is just a neurochemical con job. sometimes i feel like god made me weird and not weird like ooh she's so quirky and unique but weird like only half of what i say makes sense to people and i don't know how to express how i feel because i get choked up thinking about how people might not understand what i'm saying (and then they really don't understand what i'm saying) which is the loneliest feeling. it feels like everyone is on one wavelength and i'm on a completely different but parallel wavelength...? and i feel like i never get to talk about the things i actually want to talk about even though i talk so much. but maybe i limit my expression subconsciously because i am afraid of rejection...? 

or maybe i should give other people more credit. maybe it isn't about finding someone with the same interests and thoughts as me. maybe i should assume that the people close to me care about me a lot and would love to hold space for me, but it's on me to put myself out there first.

and if you look closely, says the tour guide, you'll notice that the artist did a horrible job in every single aspect of their work. the proportions are off, the linework is amateurish, and they clearly have no understanding of color theory. but what's truly astounding, he continues, is that it all comes together somehow - but just barely. shocked murmurs of disbelief echo among the group. the tour guide smiles gleefully - everyone is listening now. how did he do it? the tourists ask earnestly. how did one of the world's worst artists become one of the most successful charlatans of all time? how were they able to get their work displayed in one of the most exclusive galleries in the world despite their obvious lack of talent?  the tour guide nods and motions for the crowd to lean in. his voice lowers to a whisper. the artist understood something that most artists do not - that people rarely look closely. he pauses to bask in the present moment - the group is captivated by every word. and don't we all hope to get away with a certain level of deception? aren't we all afraid that if we aren't careful, those around us will see through our facade? and that the more closely we're scrutinized, the more likely they are to become disillusioned with us? 

anyways the tour guide laughs, his voice returning to its original volume. isn't most art pretentious, meaningless, and self-serving? it is nothing more than a platform for self-adoration - a constant prioritization of one's own experiences, perspectives, and motivations.

i peer through the curtains and immediately duck back down. a substantial crowd has formed on my doorstep. i look around wildly for a place to hide. the voices grow louder and more menacing. come out - we know you're in there! the leader bellows.

trembling, i cling to the doorknob and pull myself off the floor - after avoiding the townspeople for days, things have deteriorated - they are outside my house now, and it is too late to run away. show us the magic mushrooms you've been keeping in your garden, they shriek. 

but i can't i tell them. for my sake and for your sake, these mushrooms can never, ever be released for public consumption. i must guard them with my life. these mushrooms are cursed and dangerous - the potential consequences of their release are unthinkable.

well, that's subjective snaps the leader.  

the townspeople are growing more and more agitated. it is impossible to make them understand. honestly, what the hell is wrong with you? why won't you share them? your mushrooms have the potential to improve so many lives. how terribly selfish of you to keep those mushrooms to yourself. 

please, please just listen to me i beg. but their screams grow so loud that they become indistinct. 

the townspeople do not understand, and they never will. they do not realize that sharing the mushrooms will release fungal spores into the air and destroy everything. they will influence the entire hydrological cycle - our climate will never be the same. they will seep into the earth. they will contaminate our water, killing everything in it. they will poison our food. they will even affect precipitation patterns and the earth's energy budget. sharing them will be detrimental. 

get over yourself, you egoistic, self-centered, opportunistic, coward one of the townspeople scoffs. you're always so fucking dramatic. clearly, you just don't care about anyone other than yourself.

ok, harsh i sniffle. that hurt my feelings. 

i am about to cry when i realize, suddenly, that there is, in fact, a third option. i slam the front door open violently and burst out of my house, screaming at the top of my lungs. i run past the crowd, straight into the garden. i dig my fingers into the soil, frantically tearing fistfuls of magic mushrooms out of the earth, and shoving them into my pockets. clouds of spores are thrown into the air with each fistful. 

the mushrooms are heavy in my pockets, and i can feel the heat of the curses radiating from them. i have no choice. i have to get away. i run as fast as i can, the weight of the mushrooms slowing me down. my heart pounds in my chest, and my breath comes in ragged gasps. the people behind me grow distant, their shouts fading into the background. the cost is high, but i know i've done the right thing - i am now on the run with no home and nowhere to turn. the magical, cursed, and poisonous mushrooms in my pockets are a heavy burden to bear. but i have made the only choice that will keep them from wreaking havoc on the world.

we have to tiptoe, says ghostbuster. we have to live lightly, tread lightly, keep smiling, lock all the doors, close all the curtains, and never stop moving or the ghosts will find us. once they find us, they will haunt us, and they will stick to us, and we will never, ever be free of them. 

but what if it's too late? i ask nervously. what if they already found us? out of the corner of my eye, i notice a sudden movement behind the door to my right - the ghosts have found out where i live. 

then we have to laugh, says ghostbuster. as loudly as we possibly can - as if our lives depend on it...and if we're lucky, we'll fool them. as soon as we throw them off, we have to start running and we must never, ever stop. we must never slow down, even for one second. we must never turn around. we must never turn off the lights, or they will grab us by our ankles and drag us into the dark - no one will be able to save us then.
